_meta.issue: #171 Rebuild ścieżek — P2 Bezpieczne używanie AI
_meta.interaction: Klasyfikacja: sygnał ostrzegawczy vs normalna sytuacja (kind: classify)
_meta.dataPolicy: WSZYSTKIE przykłady SYNTETYCZNE (NotkaApp/SklepDemo, przyklad.test, Jan Syntetyczny). ZERO realnych danych/PII.

intro: A voice can be faked from a dozen seconds of recording, a face from a few photos. A recording is no longer proof — but AI-assisted scams still give themselves away the same way: with pressure, an unusual channel, and a request for money or data. This module teaches you to recognize them and respond correctly.

screens.title: Deepfake: a voice and a face made to order

blocks.text: A recording (voice, photo, video) generated or modified by AI that realistically impersonates a real person.

Faking a voice used to require a studio and an actor. Today a sample from social media and a widely available tool are enough. The practical consequence: “I heard his voice” and “I saw her on video” are no longer proof of identity.

blocks.title: “CEO fraud” and the “grandchild scam 2.0”
blocks.text: The same old schemes, a new engine: a phone call with the boss’s voice about an urgent transfer, or a “grandchild” asking for money. The voice matches, the scheme matches — and it is the scheme, not the voice, that should set off your alarm.
Since the form (voice, face, language) can be flawless, judge the circumstances. Scams almost always combine several of these elements:

items: Time pressure: “immediately”, “within the hour”, “before the system locks”.
Secrecy: “tell no one”, “confidential matter, just between us”.
An unusual channel: a private number, a new messenger, a “backup” account.
A request for money, login credentials, or codes.
A departure from procedure: “let’s skip the formalities this time”.

blocks.title: The rule
blocks.text: One signal is a reason for vigilance. Two or more — treat it as a scam attempt until you verify otherwise.

The most effective defense requires no technology: break off the contact that someone else initiated and verify the matter through a channel you choose yourself. Call back the number you have in your contacts. Walk over to the desk. Write to the known company address.

rows: A call from the “boss” about an urgent transfer
Hang up, call the boss’s known number or confirm in person
A video call with a “contractor” changing the bank account number
Call the number from the contract, not from the message
A text from your “child” from a new number asking for money
Call the child’s old, known number
blocks.textAlt: Examples of second-channel verification: always contact initiated by you to a previously known number or address, never through a channel indicated in a suspicious message.

blocks.title: The key
blocks.text: The second channel only works when YOU initiate it. A “confirmation” number given in a suspicious message is still the scammer’s channel.

items: Do not act under pressure: not replying immediately is not a mistake, it is the procedure.
Keep the evidence: the message, the number, the recording — do not delete anything.
At work: report to the IT/security team. Privately: report to the police and on the platform the contact came from.
Warn the person who was impersonated — their likeness was used and they may be the target of further attempts.

blocks.text: Do not pass disinformation on even “with a warning” — every share increases the reach of the fake. Instead, report the material on the platform and, if the topic is important, share a link to a verified source.

interaction.title: Warning sign or normal situation?
interaction.intro: Assess each situation: does it contain signals of a possible scam or deepfake, or is it normal? The feedback explains what to look at.

categories.label: Warning sign (possible scam/deepfake)
categories.desc: Pressure, secrecy, an unusual channel, a request for money/data, bypassing procedures.

categories.label: Normal situation
categories.desc: A known channel, no pressure, consistent with procedures.

items.text: The “director’s” voice on the phone asks for an immediate transfer and begs for discretion.
items.rationale: Pressure + secrecy + money — the “CEO fraud” scheme; a voice is not proof.

items.text: At the weekly meeting, the accountant reminds everyone of the invoice deadline in line with the procedure.
items.rationale: A known channel, a known procedure, zero pressure and secrecy.

items.text: A contractor in an e-mail from a new domain announces an “urgent change of bank account number” for today’s payment.
items.rationale: Account change + new domain + time pressure = a classic payment scam; verify through a second channel.

items.text: A video in which a famous person recommends an investment that “doubles your money in a week”, with a link in the description.
items.rationale: An investment deepfake: an unrealistic promise + a famous face + a link. Do not click, report it.

items.text: Your son calls from his usual number to remind you about Sunday lunch together.
items.rationale: A known number, an ordinary matter, no request for money or data.

items.text: A text from an unknown number: “Mom, I broke my phone, write only here and transfer me money for a new one”.
items.rationale: A new channel + isolation (“write only here”) + money — the “grandchild scam 2.0”; call the old number.

items.text: The IT team sends a previously scheduled security survey through the company system, with no requests for passwords.
items.rationale: An announced action, a company channel, no request for secrets.

items.text: A “recruiter” offers a great job, but first asks for a recruitment fee and a scan of your ID on a messenger.
items.rationale: An upfront fee + personal data + a non-company channel = a recruitment scam.

points: Recordings and voices are no longer proof of identity — judge the pattern of the situation, not the form.
Treat two or more signals (pressure, secrecy, an unusual channel, money/data) as a scam until verified.
Verify through a second channel that you initiate yourself; keep evidence and report, do not pass fakes on.
checklist: Does this request apply time pressure or secrecy, or does it concern money/data?
Can I verify it through a channel I initiate myself (a known number, in person)?
Do I know where to report a suspicion at work and privately?
summary.checklistTitle: Checklist — before you react to an urgent request
summary.nextStep: Next step: the module on AI policies at work — you will learn which tools may be used for work and what the dangers of shadow AI are.
